data.title: IBM Was 'Boring' - It's Now An AI Growth Machine
data.description:
Investment Thesis

“This is a new IBM.”

That is my five-letter advice to any investor who is aware of the inferiority of International Business Machines Corporation or IBM’s (IBM) share performance over the past 2 decades. Past returns also meant IBM’s shares were associated with being a “boring” investment.

This IBM, under current management, is swift, agile, nimble, and not afraid to switch things up—something that is extremely crucial in the age of AI, where demand cycles move at lightning speed.

For a company the size of IBM, it is exciting to see how quickly Big Blue moved to compete in the post-ChatGPT era of large language model, or LLM, wars. Now that the LLM space appears to be becoming heavily contested and commoditized, IBM is again changing its approach to compete for a growing share of high-margin enterprise IT budgets, transitioning to the platform layer of AI—agentic data. The company is doing this without taking its eyes off the pedal of its free cash engine.

I continue to maintain my bullish view on IBM and explain my thesis below.

IBM’s New AI Strategy Targets The Real Currency Of AI

IBM currently has two growth ramps that are driving the company towards becoming a beneficiary of emerging technologies—AI & Quantum. I discussed IBM’s quantum compute strategy in August last year, and the shares are up ~40% since then.

Over the past 2-3 months I have noticed Big Blue making quiet but strong moves towards shifting their approach towards AI.

In the post-ChatGPT era, so far, IBM’s approach towards AI has been to compete in the market for frontier LLMs by leveraging an open-source strategy, particularly through its Granite series of models. The open-source strategy was based on its Red Hat open-source platform, which has been one of IBM’s most transformative acquisitions thus far, in my view. Apart from IBM, Meta Platforms (META) was the other significant player that competed in the LLM landscape via an open-source adoption strategy.

That strategy worked well for IBM, as seen in the chart below, where IBM Software, the business segment that manages the development of products and platforms for most AI initiatives, went from growing at a low-to-mid single-digit pace to a high single-digit pace. The acceleration in IBM’s Software segment also helped Big Blue grow at a mid-high single-digit quarterly growth pace, much better than the low-mid single-digit growth pace between 2023 and 2024. (The current growth pace impressively eclipses the period of contracting revenues in the previous decade.)

However, in the past 15-18 months, competition in the LLM landscape has become fierce, leading to heavy commoditization and a significant drop in LLM inference prices. In such situations, open-source approaches might yield desired results, pushing leading players to rethink the open-source approach and/or the pursuit of LLM dominance altogether.

I believe IBM’s management understood the trade-off between staying committed on their increasingly competitive journey towards LLM dominance and finding newer higher-margin, value play areas where they could stay competitive within the broader LLM landscape. This probably explains why IBM’s exec team switched tactics towards competing in the platform ecosystem for LLMs and made two acquisitions in the automation and data platform end market for LLMs along with the HashiCorp acquisition last year.

In the chart below, I break out IBM’s Software segment by its subsegments and plot the annual growth rates per quarter for each Software subsegment. Investors will notice two things.

First, both the acquisitions that IBM made in the last 18 months were shrewd additions to the company, accretive to the company’s overall growth, as seen above. Second, the timing of both these acquisitions appears to be almost perfect, with the company quickly folding the acquisitions into the company’s Software business segment, rolling it out to customers, and rapidly benefiting from the synergy of these acquisitions made.

I believe IBM’s management may have been satisfied with how synergetic the two 2024 acquisitions were to IBM Software’s Data business. But the fact that growth was not able to match up with the teens' growth rates seen in Red Hat and, especially, in Automation, may have pushed management to re-energize their Data platform with the recent acquisition of data streaming product company Confluent. In my view, this is a clever acquisition by IBM’s management because it will allow the company to address the data streaming and orchestration market that will become more important as the competition and complexity at the LLM layer proliferate further. IBM is now appearing to target the exabytes of data being ingested by LLMs, which is the real currency for LLMs.

I suspect IBM fully understands the extent to which the company can sell to strong demand and price to value based on the synergy of its Confluent acquisition. This is why management already divulged that they expect the Confluent acquisition to be “accretive to adjusted EBITDA within the first full year, and free cash flow in year two, post close.”

IBM’s recent history suggests that management usually has a good track record with its acquisitions. If we factor in management’s guidance from the Confluent acquisition at face value, then it's highly likely that IBM’s margins will continue to soar despite the company delivering to investors their highest-ever margin profile.

That robust margin expansion has allowed IBM to deliver three successive years of free cash growth. Currently, the company is on track to deliver >5% free cash growth, amounting to ~$14B. Many investors may still shy away from the single-digit growth rates in IBM’s revenue and free cash. Rather than focusing on the digit growth rates, I would implore investors to focus on the acceleration or accretion, which creates incremental investability in IBM’s shares.

My take—that only means there likely is a stronger update coming in IBM’s FCF outlook next week when the company reports Q4 CY25 earnings or the next quarterly earnings reports.

IBM’s Valuation Still Points To Upside

As detailed in one of my earlier coverages on IBM, I will continue to believe that IBM can deliver free cash growth rates of 5.9%. I am mostly inclined to upgrade this outlook if the recent acquisitions are accretive to free cash growth, but I will wait for management’s outlook and forward-looking views from next week’s ER (January 28th).

Presently, markets are pricing IBM’s shares in a manner that is consistent with expectations of 4.8% free cash growth, per my reverse DCF model. Since I have already stated my expectations of 5.9% free cash growth, my reverse DCF model prices IBM’s shares at ~$329 per share. The reverse DCF assumes a discount rate of 7.6%.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

IBM’s new strategy to gradually pivot away from a focus on LLMs towards the data and AI platform ecosystem is not entirely new, but there is a risk the strategy may not pay off if competition in the space significantly rises from here. Apart from DataBricks, Snowflake (SNOW) and other vendors such as Amazon (AMZN) AWS’s Kinesis and Alphabet (GOOG) Google Cloud’s Dataflow are in this space. IBM’s first focus would be to expand its current book of business of $9.5B since inception by selling to its current installed base of customers.

With Q4 ER on tap next week, here’s what investors can expect:

  • Q4 EPS of $4.3 growing 9.5% on Q4 revenues of $19.23B growing 9.6%. That implies a full-year CY25 EPS of $11.35, up 9.9% on CY25 revenues of $67.1B, up 6.7%.
  • For CY26, markets are expecting a slowdown in revenues of 4.5% growth to $70.2B, which is conservative, in my opinion.
  • IBM Consulting/Managed Services will be another area to watch for investors to understand if the industry slowdown is still likely to persist in the new year.

Takeaway

With another widely expected year of revenue acceleration, IBM has emerged from its decades-long shadow of revenue contractions, firmly capitalizing on the strong demand it sees for its AI platform. The gradual move away from competing in LLMs to being the platform provider marks a clever shift for the company, positioning itself to deliver free cash growth in the face of structural AI demand.

I continue to reiterate my bullishness for IBM’s shares.

data.title: Deciding 'When' To Buy
data.description:
This is an educational article. All examples are for educational purposes and are not a direct commendation except as stated.

Deciding “what to buy” is different than deciding “when” to buy it. Warren Buffett highlighted this when he said: “A simple rule dictates my buying. Be fearful when others are greedy, and be greedy when others are fearful.” Notice his “when” is based on investor emotions not economic data.

Yet, we know his decisions on “what to buy” are based on analyzing a company’s economic situation compared to its current value or price.

It’s important to understand these are two separate and distinct decisions.

Opinion: I want to make something clear. Deciding “what to buy” should be determined by careful analysis of a company’s industry and the economics of that company. That’s what most of the analysis at Seeking Alpha is about. However, in the area of deciding “when to buy” fundamental analysis is not very good and investor sentiment is by far the better tool.

Investor Sentiment

Investor “fear” and “greed” falls under the broad category of investor sentiment. Another comparable term is “investor expectations.”

There are numerous ways to measure investor sentiment or expectations in an individual stock but by far the best way is through puts and calls data. It's a direct way to measure how bullish or bearish investors are about that stock.

Our analysis compares the amount of money going into directional puts and calls over twenty days. This time frame gives the data a long term perspective.

We then put the results on the Sentiment King Red Zone Green Zone ranking method, which graphically shows when bullish or bearish expectations are at extremes. I recently wrote about it in this article (New option indicators are signaling higher prices).

Example: T-Mobile

Now the example company I'm going to use in the article is T-Mobile (TMUS). Here is its seven-year chart. As you can see, it had a serious setback last year.

Suppose, after careful economic analysis of T-Mobile and its sector, which is telecommunications, one has decided that this is the company they want to buy for the long term. Question: Is this the right time to buy it?

It’s answering this question where investor sentiment can help the analyst.

We’re first going to look at investor sentiment in the telecommunications sector. Then we’re going to look at investor sentiment in T-Mobile itself.

Sectors

First, let's take a look at the sector, which is telecommunications. Here's a chart of how the sector ranks on our sentiment scale for the major S&P sectors.

We’ve circled telecommunications in red. Notice that the telecommunications sector ranks as the second most extreme in investor expectations.

It's always positive when you have extreme bearish expectations. This is why Buffett likes to buy when investors are negative or fearful.

The Telecommunications Sector

This next chart gives a closer look at why this sector registers at one of the most extreme Green Zone readings.

The price chart graphs the State Street telecommunications ETF. The bottom curve is our ranking of the directional puts and calls ratio over 20 days of all 49 stocks that make up this sector.

Notice how past Green Zone readings were good moments to buy the sector and bad moments to sell it. Likewise, Red Zone areas were good moments to generally sell or moments to wait for a better time to buy it.

It's important to note that sentiment is an intermediate term indicator, not a long term forecasting tool. The directional premium puts to call ratio generally produces signals every few months.

So this chart tells an analyst, if they're suddenly optimistic about prospects for telecommunications, that now is a good time to buy the sector.

It also tells an analyst if they're negative on the sector, they should probably wait before they sell a stock in that sector. That they most likely will get a higher price to sell at.

Investor Sentiment In T-Mobile

How is investor sentiment in T-Mobile itself? Here is a corresponding chart for T-Mobile. The bottom curve ranks the directed puts to calls premium ratio over the last 20 days.

While there are exceptions, Green Zone readings were generally good buying opportunities while red zone readings were good selling opportunities.

After the yearlong decline, investor sentiment is the most negative it’s been in years. For investors who are bullish on T-Mobile, this indicates that it's a good time to purchase.

For those investors who are negative on the outlook for T-Mobile, it means that they should wait and get a better price before they sell.

Do You Always Wait For "Fear" Or "Greed" To Act?

Does an investor only act when there's greed or fear? Does Warren Buffett only act when there's greed or fear? No. He just buys more when there is fear and buys less when there is greed. Remember, the extreme moments he's referring to don't occur that often.

When sentiment is not at an extreme, which is most of the time, and an investor is bullish or bearish on a stock, they should go ahead and act. You don't have to wait for extreme sentiment, a Red Zone or Green Zone signal, to buy or sell.

However, if sentiment is at an extreme, we think you should act or wait to get a better price depending on the situation.

Summary

So, I would never buy a stock I was optimistic about without first looking at investor sentiment to see if I might get a better price if I wait to buy.

Likewise, I would never sell a company I was negative on without first looking at whether it's in the Green Zone, and I'll probably get a better price if I wait there too.

Sentiment can help an investor who has decided to buy or sell get the best price that aligns with their decision.

data.title: Nomura High Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

US high yield bonds, as measured by the ICE BofA US Cash Pay High Yield Index, returned +1.35% for 4Q25, while European and global high yield bonds returned +0.52% and +1.32%, as measured by the ICE BofA Euro High Yield Index and the ICE BofA US Dollar Global High Yield Index, respectively.

The ICE BofA US Cash Pay High Yield Index returned +0.20% in October as concerns about third-quarter earnings reports and a possible US government shutdown triggered a flight to quality for high yield market participants. Given the shift in investor risk appetite, BB-rated securities outperformed lower-quality B-rated and CCC-rated securities. BB-rated securities returned +0.50% for the quarter versus declines of 0.06% and 0.37% for B-rated and CCC-rated securities, respectively.

High yield market volatility continued into November as the US government shutdown and the increased likelihood of US Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts resulted in another strong quarter of performance from the higher-quality component of the high yield market. In November, the ICE BofA US Cash Pay High Yield Index returned +0.50% as BB-rated securities returned +0.63% followed by B-rated securities returning +0.70% and CCC-rated securities returning declining 0.76%. This was the first time since October 2023 in which CCC-rated bonds declined two consecutive months.

December closed the year strong as positive economic data points, a dovish Fed, and robust high yield capital markets activity provided the backdrop for risk assets to perform well. CCC-rated bonds ended their two-month streak of negative returns by returning +0.72% for the month. B-rated bonds also performed very well, leading the way with a +0.90% return for the month followed by BB-rated bonds returning 0.47% for the same period.

For the quarter, BB-rated bonds outperformed, returning +1.58%, while B-rated and CCC-rated bonds returned +1.55% and -0.42%, respectively. The leading sectors were broadcasting (+3.57%), telecommunications (+2.74%), and metals/mining (+2.67%). The weakest-performing sectors were retail (-0.75%), media (-0.67%), and paper/packaging (-0.62%).

New-issue volume totaled $65.4 billion for the quarter. For the full year 2025, refinancings comprised 70.4% of new-issue activity, followed by mergers and acquisitions at 13.9% and general corporate purposes at 14.8%. The 12-month par-weighted US high yield default rate ended the quarter at 1.88% and the outlook for the 2026 default rate ranges from 1.75% to 2.25%.

Sources: Bloomberg and J.P. Morgan, unless otherwise noted.

Within the Fund

For 4Q25, Nomura High Income Fund Institutional Class (IVHIX) shares slightly underperformed the Fund’s benchmark, the ICE BofA US High Yield Constrained Index.

Key contributors to performance included:

  • Security selection within the media sector
  • Security selection within the retail sector
  • Security selection within the automotive sector
  • Security selection within the banking sector

Key detractors from performance included:

  • Security selection within the leisure sector
  • An underweight to the real estate sector
  • An underweight to the healthcare sector
  • Security selection within the consumer goods sector

Outlook

Looking forward, we expect potential bouts of volatility. Earnings could come under stress if tariff-related issues and weaker consumer spending weigh on corporate margins and free cash flow profiles. Nevertheless, solid corporate balance sheets, low default rates, and attractive yields should help mitigate upside risks to yields and spreads within the high yield market. We will continue to rely on our disciplined, bottom-up approach to capitalize on opportunities as valuations warrant.

The performance quoted represents past performance and does not guarantee future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. Performance data current to the most recent month end may be obtained by calling 800 523-1918 or visiting nomuraassetmanagement.com/performance.

Total returns may reflect waivers and/or expense reimbursements by the manager and/or distributor (as applicable) for some or all of the periods shown. Performance would have been lower without such waivers and reimbursements.

Performance at NAV assumes that no front-end sales charge applied. Performance at offer assumes that a front-end sales charge applied to the extent applicable. As of December 1, 2025, Nomura Holding America Inc. completed the acquisition of Macquarie Asset Management’s US and European public investments business.

Carefully consider the Fund’s investment objectives, risk factors, charges, and expenses before investing. This and other information can be found in the Fund’s prospectus and its summary prospectus, which may be obtained by visiting nomuraassetmanagement.com/literature or calling 800 523-1918. Investors should read the prospectus and the summary prospectus carefully before investing.

The views expressed represent the investment team’s assessment of the Fund and market environment as of the date indicated, and should not be considered a recommendation to buy, hold, or sell any security, and should not be relied on as research or investment advice.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal.

A fund or strategy may not receive payment of principal, interest, and other amounts due in connection with investments in loans and other direct indebtedness. Furthermore, loans and other direct indebtedness may be less liquid than other investments and may not be considered securities that are subject to the protections of securities laws.

Fixed income securities can lose value, including the possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities are subject to credit risk and interest rate risk. Credit risk is the risk that an issuer of a fixed income security may be unable to make interest payments and/or repay principal in a timely manner. Interest rate risk is the risk that prices of bonds and other fixed income securities will increase as interest rates fall and decrease as interest rates rise. Fixed income securities with longer maturities or duration generally are more sensitive to interest rate changes. • Fluctuations in exchange rates between currencies may cause the value of an investment to decline. Some currencies may have low liquidity which may prevent the fund from effecting positions at favorable rates, thus subjecting the fund or strategy to substantial losses. • Foreign securities (particularly in emerging markets) may be adversely affected by political instability, changes in currency exchange rates, inefficient markets and higher transaction costs, foreign economic conditions, the imposition of economic or trade sanctions, or inadequate or different regulatory and accounting standards. • High yield securities (“junk bonds”) are subject to reduced creditworthiness of issuers, increased risk of default, and a more limited and less liquid secondary market. High yield securities may also be subject to greater price volatility and risk of loss of income and principal than higher-rated securities. • The fund or strategy is subject to liquidity risk, which is the risk that the fund or strategy’s investments cannot be readily sold within seven calendar days at approximately the price at which the fund or strategy has valued them. • The principal on a fixed income security may be prepaid prior to maturity, which may require reinvestment at a lower interest rate. • Restricted securities may be less liquid and harder to value than unrestricted securities.

Index performance returns do not reflect any management fees, transaction costs, or expenses. Indices are unmanaged and one cannot invest directly in an index. The ICE BofA US High Yield Constrained Index tracks the performance of US dollar-denominated high yield corporate debt publicly issued in the US domestic market, but caps individual issuer exposure at 2% of the benchmark. The ICE BofA Euro High Yield Index tracks the performance of euro-denominated below-investment-grade corporate debt publicly issued in the euro domestic or Eurobond markets. The ICE BofA US Cash Pay High Yield Index tracks the performance of US dollar-denominated below-investment-grade corporate debt, currently in a coupon paying period, that is publicly issued in the US domestic market. Qualifying securities must have at least 18 months to final maturity at the time of issuance, at least one-year remaining term to final maturity as of the rebalancing date, a fixed coupon schedule, and a minimum amount outstanding of $250 million. The ICE BofA US Dollar Global High Yield Index tracks the performance of US dollar-denominated below-investment-grade corporate debt publicly issued in the US domestic and Eurobond markets.

This document may mention bond ratings published by nationally recognized statistical rating organizations (NRSROs) Standard & Poor’s, Moody’s Investors Service, and Fitch, Inc. Bonds rated AAA are rated as having the highest quality and are generally considered to have the lowest degree of investment risk. Bonds rated AA are considered to be of high quality, but with a slightly higher degree of risk than bonds rated AAA. Bonds rated A are considered to have many favorable investment qualities, though they are somewhat more susceptible to adverse economic conditions. Bonds rated BBB are believed to be of medium-grade quality and generally riskier over the long term. Bonds rated BB, B, and CCC are regarded as having significant speculative characteristics, with BB indicating the least degree of speculation of the three.

Institutional Class shares, Class R shares, Class R6 shares, and Class Y shares are available only to certain investors. See the prospectus for more information. Nothing presented should be construed as a recommendation to purchase or sell any security or follow any investment technique or strategy.

All third-party marks cited are the property of their respective owners.

Nomura Asset Management is part of the Investment Management Division of the Nomura Group, providing integrated public and private market asset management services across equities, fixed income, private credit and multi-asset solutions to intermediary and institutional clients. Nomura Asset Management primarily operates through several distinct investment managers, which includes Nomura Investment Management Business Trust (NIMBT), a Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) registered investment adviser. Investment advisory services are provided to the Nomura Funds by Delaware Management Company, a series of NIMBT. The Nomura Funds are distributed by Delaware Distributors, L.P., a registered broker/dealer and member of the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA) and an affiliate of NIMBT.

data.title: The Hidden Robot
data.description:
By Rafael Silva

Be it Wall-E, R2-D2, or Rosie from The Jetsons, most readers grew up with sci-fi molding what a robot looks like and is capable of doing. Technology has now come so far that we no longer need to rely on Hollywood to imagine this part of the future for us. News shows and viral videos exposing us to actual hardware being developed today carry that mantle now.

We’ve moved from the imaginary to the technically possible, but most of our knowledge remains founded on what is fun to watch, rather than what is making a true difference to society. After all, Boston Dynamic’s dancing dogs and humanoids get more views than Fanuc's (FANUY) robots, which are involved in the production of a significant amount of all vehicles globally and have shipped over a million units.

The Invisible Army Behind the Scenes

There’s an army of robots marching behind the scenes to enable today’s lifestyle. We can divide those into industrial and service robots. FANUC is an example of the first. The industrial robot market is the larger and vastly more mature of the two. It contributes to the manufacturing of electronics, automotives, machinery, as well as behind-the-scenes logistics automation happening in warehouses across the globe. This makes it less visible to the average person.

Service robots, on the other hand, are much more likely to coexist with us. We may interact with service robots as a user or as a “colleague” of sorts. We would see these in areas like last-mile logistics, professional cleaning, or healthcare, for example. This proximity to a larger part of the population still doesn’t provide the average person with an insight into the growth or the prevalence of the technology. How many of us are aware that the number of industrial robots in operation has increased 216% in the last 10 years? It’s gone from 1.5 million in 2014 to 4.7 million in 2024.

Service Robots and Labor Realities

The latest available data from the International Federation for Robotics (IFR) estimates that 2024 saw a total of 199,000 professional service robots installed, a 9% increase from the previous year. That number, while robust in itself, does not tell the whole story. Within the service robots category, the installation of medical robots increased by 91% to 16,700 units. Meanwhile, professional cleaning robots saw their installations increase by 34%, and search and rescue increased by 19%. These numbers give us an indication of two things. First of all, what tasks the current technology is capable of performing; second, and most importantly, what industries face the biggest labor shortages.

To determine whether a task is automated, ask whether a robot would be more efficient in the long term. Essentially, this automation deployment happens where economics meet both functionality and scalability. Importantly, new drivers for automation are coming into play on the demand side. There, we have an aging population and a lack of skilled workers as primary drivers. The World Health Organization (WHO) is projecting a global shortage of over 10 million healthcare workers by 2030, for example. That is no doubt part of the reason why healthcare is making a strong push for automation today.

Healthcare as a Window Into Automation

Healthcare provides us with a great window into how these forces are interacting. 2024 saw an increase of 610% in the installation of diagnostics & medical laboratory analysis robots (IFR data). These robots allow for precise and efficient execution of time-consuming and error-prone tasks, 24/7. Rehabilitation & non-invasive therapy (+106%) as well as surgical (+41%) robots are another example of segments where the machines are quickly providing high returns on investment. They bring either immediate efficiency improvements or enable the available workforce to do a better job in an environment less prone to burnout.

The existing technology continuously improves, fueled by advances in materials and software. Meanwhile, this same progress is opening doors to new types of robots. Last year’s addition to the ROBO Global Robotics and Automation Index (ROBO), Ondas Holdings (ONDS), is deploying mine swiping as well as border patrol robots, among many other solutions for autonomous strategic asset protection. Drones were brought into focus during the ongoing Ukrainian conflict. They very quickly moved from niche consumer market to an important part of defense, agriculture and even transportation.

Humanoids and the Household Challenge

Humanoids also exploded into the scene in 2025. They brought the enormous potential of deployment in a world built for humans and follow a trend of integrating standalone technologies to create synergies. Cleaning robots can also provide an element of security. Hotel robots capable of carrying food or towels are also capable of providing directions or answering questions about hotel amenities. Essentially, we are entering an age of general-purpose robots.

Going into 2026, it is important to note that the accelerated growth seen in the robotics industry has been against a backdrop of uncertain political policies and high cost of capital. While neither of these is conducive to high capex investments like robotic installations, there have been billions of capital commitments for reshoring in the U.S. and abroad. Industrial use cases are increasing and diversifying, and adoption among the service industry is growing rapidly. We are seeing fast adoption of models such as robotics-as-a-service (RaaS) as well as increasingly sophisticated financing models. Of note, in the United States we will see continued benefit of 100% bonus depreciation of capex investments in the first year.

One place where growth has been limited is the home. As of today, robotic vacuums remain the only product to successfully make it into our houses. Humanoids may change that. Incredible pent-up and anticipated demand for multipurpose robots capable of doing chores around the house remains. The challenges to that remain the same they have been from the beginning: availability, safety and cost.

The breakthroughs driving humanoid performance don’t exist in isolation. Vision systems, manipulation algorithms, and AI models transfer readily across form factors, suggesting households may adopt multiple specialized robots rather than just a single general-purpose unit. The question shifts from when to how many.

The Geopolitical Race for Leadership

Going forward, we see the robotic industry as one poised for rapid growth. The question of where that growth will come from - and what companies will lead it - is a much more complex one. At this point, China is undoubtedly leading the adoption race, with over half of all industrial installations taking place in the country. They also have about 100 companies actively developing humanoid robots. The U.S., on the other hand, is undergoing the third phase of its National Robotics Initiative (NRI-3.0). It announced its Genesis Mission late in 2025, described by the administration as a “Manhattan project for AI and robotics.” This creates a complex environment where governmental policy is as important as technological development by individual companies.

While growth may be likely, picking the winners requires expertise. That is why the ROBO Global Robotics and Automation Index (ROBO) relies on a strategic advisory board including the actual architects of the robotics revolution. People like Dr. Daniela Rus, the Director of MIT CSAIL and Dr. Henrik Christensen, professor of computer science at UC San Diego, represent part of a team of market and thought leaders which help the strategy identify the invisible robots that are actually building the future.

ROBO is the underlying index for the ROBO Global Robotics & Automation ETF (ROBO), the L&G ROBO Global Robotics and Automation UCITS ETF (ROBO.LN), and the Global X ROBO Global Robotics & Automation ETF (ROBO.AU).

Disclosure: © VettaFi LLC 2026. All rights reserved. This material has been prepared and/or issued by VettaFi LLC ("VettaFi") and/or one of its consultants or affiliates. It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit www.vettafi.com

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Jabil: Rating Upgrade On Better Revenue Visibility And Competitive Position
data.description:
Summary

I gave a hold rating to Jabil Inc. (JBL) previously, with my worry being whether JBL can execute given the capacity bottlenecks to justify its premium valuation. So far, the developments have changed my view. Data center architecture is shifting in JBL's favor, revenue visibility has improved meaningfully, and management has taken steps to remove the key bottlenecks that limited execution. I upgrade to buy.

Structural Shifts Favor Jabil's Model

A few months back, my view was less bullish on JBL because I saw execution risk. While AI spending was accelerating, JBL's growth was constrained by capacity (from power to labor to even chips). But things have changed. There is a structural shift in data center architecture that materially improves JBL's positioning, I believe. The industry is moving away from traditional top-of-rack switches toward independent switching racks, which is a better design to support GPU-heavy workloads. This is a massive change that effectively rewired the value chain in the data center, which is where JBL's years of investments in its Intelligent Infrastructure strategy (built around complex rack-level assembly, power integration, and large-scale electromechanical systems) will start to shine.

As you can imagine, the complexity and value proposition change. JBL is no longer competing within the "box," where it is really a competition of assembling the right components to ensure the "box" works. When we are talking about "rack," the competition is on who can best integrate, test, and deliver full rack-level solutions at scale. That capability is way harder to replicate vs. box-level manufacturing. The comparison is simple to understand. When you build a switch, you really only need a workbench and a conveyor line. When you integrate a rack for modern AI workloads, you are effectively assembling a high-performance computing cluster inside a cabinet. That requires reinforced concrete floors to support the weight (can exceed 2,500 lbs per rack), and you also need liquid cooling infrastructure running through the facility (to test and make sure the rack doesn't melt) and megawatts of power capacity (to conduct burn-in and validation testing). Most ODMs that focused only on the "box" don't have the infrastructure for this. It is not like they can simply turn this capability on overnight. They are literally restrained by physical requirements, which creates a real, physical barrier to entry. This is why I think JBL is in a very solid position to win a ton of share (or in other words, its addressable opportunity got larger).

Not only that, but JBL also has an advantage when it comes to partnership with Nvidia (NVDA). Given that JBL has already had a manufacturing partnership with NVDA for a while now, it is one of the scaled players outside of East Asia that can meet this ramp as NVDA aims to reduce geopolitical risk. From NVDA's perspective, that makes JBL's harder to replace, which effectively embeds JBL deeper into NVDA's product roadmap.

Revenue Visibility Improves

Another reason I stayed on the sidelines previously was visibility. At the time, backlog was building, but it was not translating into recognized revenue fast enough to justify the valuation. I wanted to wait until demand showed up in the income statement before I got interested in turning bullish. JBL did just that, and it is stronger than I expected.

On visibility, management raised FY2026 AI revenue guidance to $12.1 billion, up from $11.2 billion previously. That is near an entire billion dollars of upgrade, which sends a strong message that the backlog is going to translate into revenue growth and suggests the constraints I worried about (component availability and customer acceptance delays) are likely easing. This materially improves forward visibility. Two reasons I think the guide is plausible. First is that management guidance has historically been accurate (~1.5% delta on average).

The second is because JBL has effectively "bought" the certainty through the Hanley Energy acquisition. This literally solves the single biggest friction point in the bear case (capacity constraints). I used to be in that camp worrying about JBL's needs to secure the power and cooling supply, which could swing intra-quarter numbers, as JBL may not be able to meet the client's (especially hyperscalers) aggressive delivery schedules. Hanley Energy is the piece that solves this puzzle. For reference, Hanley Energy specializes in power management solutions for data centers. By bringing this capability in-house, Jabil has vertically integrated the most critical choke point (today) in the AI infrastructure buildout. In the past, JBL wasn't able to recognize revenue because of supply constraints, as it relies on third-party suppliers. If a power unit vendor was late, JBL couldn't ship the rack, and revenue slipped to the next quarter. By vertically integrating, JBL now has better control of the timing of the entire assembly process. This should reduce the risk of shipment delays, making the revenue timing much more predictable.

While the price tag is huge, so much so that it required JBL to issue a $1 billion senior notes offering split between 2029 and 2033 maturities, I believe the strategic rationale outweighs the cost of capital. I see this as a relatively cheap way to finance a capability that can enable JBL to deliver the $12.1 billion revenue target. If JBL had not made this move, they would continue to be at the mercy of third-party vendors during a period of peak demand. From an offensive standpoint, by integrating Hanley, JBL not only protects its delivery schedule but also captures more margin dollars per rack. It also enhances JBL's value proposition to hyperscalers, who are desperate for vendors that can provide turnkey solutions.

Valuation

Given that my concerns are basically addressed, I now believe JBL is worth a buy rating. You can see from the chart above that consensus has upgraded their estimates (adj. EPS growth up ~800 bps vs. my update in September '25). The market is clearly resonating with my views, as multiples have gone higher, now at 20x vs. ~19x NTM PE back then. But I think it makes sense, and I believe multiple can stay at this level so long as JBL can continue to ride on this AI infrastructure build-out phase.

JBL has almost never missed consensus adj. EPS estimates before, for reference, and I don't think it will anytime soon given the visibility it has. Looking out into the next few years, JBL is expected to generate ~$15.9 adj. EPS. If multiple stays at 20x, the stock is easily worth more than $300, or more than 20% upside from here.

Investment Risk

Integrating the Hanley acquisition may be harder than expected. Any friction in merging power products could ultimately lead to a delay in deliveries and, hence, revenue recognition. While I think it makes sense to use debt, it does increase leverage, which could weigh on multiples if JBL doesn't deliver. Also, any pause/delay in hyperscaler AI capex spending would also hurt JBL's revenue growth cadence.

Conclusion

My view for JBL is a buy rating. The structural tailwinds from rack-level data center architecture, deeper ties with NVDA, and materially improved revenue visibility have addressed my previous concerns. The Hanley Energy acquisition directly removes the most important constraint, making delivery timing and revenue conversion far more visible. While leverage has increased, I believe the trade-off is justified.

data.title: Grid Dynamics: Focus On Inorganic Moves (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I'm becoming bullish on Grid Dynamics Holdings (GDYN).

GDYN's external growth prospects are healthy, considering recent news flow and the company's financial strength.

My prior May 8, 2024, write-up touched on its better-than-anticipated turnover and below-consensus bottom line for 1Q24.

Positive On Alliances With Tech Heavyweights

Last month, GDYN revealed a "multi-year strategic collaboration agreement (SCA) with Amazon (AMZN) Web Services (AWS)."

Ecosystem Members

My take is that this latest development will help maintain the group's competitiveness.

The GDYN-AMZN tie-up spans one and a half decades. The company's website states that its "AWS start-up kit" can "reduce time-to-value for generative AI initiatives by up to 60%."

You would worry about the firm losing substantial business without Amazon's support.

A newly added partner is Nvidia (NVDA).

NVDA-GDYN "Intralogistics Optimization Starter Kit" Introduced Last Year

I think that its allies are important in many ways.

At the 3Q25 briefing, GDYN highlighted "joint marketing and sales campaigns" with "the hyperscalers." These initiatives allow it to capitalize on the huge sales teams of Microsoft (MSFT) Azure and Google (GOOG) Cloud to attract customers.

Also, it has gained acceptance from reputable research organizations in recent years, as outlined below. That was made possible by the co-developed solutions. Its slides indicated that the "40+ proprietary accelerators", mostly created together with GOOG/AMZN, can "reduce Fortune 1000 implementation time from 12+ months to 6-9 months."

GDYN's Awards

GDYN saw its list of collaborators expand from a mid-single-digit to the low teens in the last 36 months.

That led to the proportion of the enterprise's partner-linked topline rising from under a percent to 18% (source: Q3 call) during the same period.

As per S&P Capital IQ, the sell-side is projecting that GDYN's revenue will accelerate from FY22-25's +9.9% to FY2025-2028's +13.5%. Analysts also estimate a +140bps (basis points) EBITDA-to-sales improvement between '24 and '28.

The market's optimism is reasonable in my view.

The company has a goal of raising the partner-related contribution to 25%-30%. That should enhance its future turnover.

Separately, GDYN indicated at the 3Q2025 meeting it often acts as "Client Zero" by utilizing partner-supported AI tools to enhance "efficiency on our own business process." This points to lower expenses going forward.

Acquisitions Are In The Cards

Morgan Stanley (MS) published a report (not publicly accessible) titled "Investment Banks Deal Dashboard" on Dec. 26, 2025. MS's research suggested that worldwide "announced M&A volume" surged +65% YoY in 4Q25. The same metric for North America is even higher at +119%.

I believe that this M&A wave is sustainable.

An early-January 2026 SA News piece cited a "Citizens Financial Group (CFG) survey" of "U.S.-based companies" and "PE firms." The results suggest "90% expect deal flow will rise or hold steady this year, due to improving economic conditions and attractive sector-wide valuations."

There are internal factors compelling GDYN to follow this trend.

The firm's net cash as of Sept. 30, 2025, amounted to $0.33 billion. That was equivalent to 45% of its market cap.

It has been free cashflow positive for every single year since FY2017 based on S&P Capital IQ data. I don't see the need for GDYN to hold such a sizeable amount of surplus capital.

GDYN's Past Takeovers

Also, it hasn't done any deals in the past 16 months. The previous ones were mainly bolt-ons, with the investees boasting annual revenues of under $0.05B.

GDYN stressed at the 3Q analyst briefing that "M&A continues to take priority in our capital allocation strategy." It also mentioned that the focus is on "acquisitions that strategically enhance our capabilities, geographic presence and industry verticals."

I infer from the management's commentary that the acquisition drought could be over soon. It is also likely to look at more sizable targets, which can boost its growth outlook. The cash pile available for deployment should imply a greater deal appetite.

The 13.5% top-line CAGR projection referenced in the preceding section might be surpassed in the presence of inorganic growth.

You Should Contemplate These Bear-Case Scenarios

If key allies choose to work with GDYN's rivals instead, there's a risk of market share loss.

The company might continue to engage in cash-hoarding. In that case, investors will most probably assign a hefty valuation discount to penalize the stock for poor capital allocation.

My Concluding Remarks

I've revised GDYN's rating from "Hold" to "Buy."

I like how it's expanding through external means.

The stock is still valued using a "Price-to-Earnings Growth/PEG" method as per the May '24 article.

GDYN trades at 18.5x FY26 P/E. The consensus FY25-28 EPS CAGR estimate is +22.1% (source: S&P Capital IQ).

Its potential upside is +20% (22.1/18.5) pegged to 1xPEG, justifying a favorable investment view.

data.title: Hartford Core Equity Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

US equities advanced in the fourth quarter, registering a sizable return for 2025. Markets were bolstered by robust corporate earnings, resilient consumer spending, and solid economic growth, although anxiety about lofty valuations in the technology sector and concerns surrounding massive AI spending drove capital toward value/cyclical areas of the market. The cooling labor market and worries about upside inflation risks also kept the markets on edge. In the third quarter, the US economy grew at the fastest pace in two years; GDP surged 4.3% annually, primarily fueled by robust consumer spending but also aided by a rebound in exports, business investment on equipment and AI, and increased government spending on defense. The US Federal Reserve (Fed) reduced interest rates by 50 basis points (bps)* during the quarter. Policymakers were divided on their outlook for interest rates amid considerable uncertainty about inflation and the labor market, with the Fed’s median dot-plot projection showing only one rate cut in 2026.

Performance Summary

  • Hartford Core Equity Fund (I share) underperformed the S&P 500 Index during the quarter.
  • Security selection detracted from performance driven by weak selection in information technology, materials, and communications services. This was partially offset by strong selection in industrials, healthcare, and consumer staples.
  • Sector allocation, a residual of our bottom-up stock selection process, also detracted from performance driven by an underweight to healthcare. This was partially offset by an underweight to information technology.
  • Top relative detractors included an overweight in Pool Corp (consumer discretionary), not owning Micron Technology (information technology), and an overweight in AutoZone (consumer discretionary).

Positioning & Outlook

  • The AI infrastructure capex cycle will remain a key 2026 theme, but is evolving from broad enthusiasm toward a more discerning focus on capital intensity and funding needs. The year should also mark a shift from pure AI enablers to companies best positioned to translate AI adoption into revenue growth, efficiency, and margin expansion across sectors. The US consumer warrants continued monitoring as affordability pressures persist. Wage growth has moderated, labor-market cooling is evident, and spending patterns are shifting. While early 2026 may benefit from seasonal tax-related stimulus, cumulative inflation continues to weigh on households, with autos and housing remaining key drivers of sentiment and electoral outcomes. We remain disciplined and focused on companies with improving quality and fundamental momentum. We favor businesses with pricing power, strong balance sheets, and operational agility—attributes that help navigate tariff passthroughs and policy volatility.
  • At the end of the period, our largest overweights were to consumer staples and utilities. The largest underweights were to healthcare and information technology.
*Basis point is a unit that is equal to 1/100th of 1% and is used to denote the change in a financial instrument. The basis point is commonly used for calculating changes in interest rates, equity indexes and the yield of a fixed-income security

The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For more current performance information to the most recent month ended, please visit hartfordfunds.com.

Had fees and expenses of a class been reflected for the periods prior to the inception of that class, performance would be different. Since inception (SI) performance is from 4/30/98. Performance and expenses for other share classes will vary. Additional information is in the prospectus. Only Class A assesses a sales charge. Net expenses reflect such arrangements only with respect to Class I. These arrangements remain in effect until 2/28/26 unless the Fund's Board of Directors approves an earlier termination. Without these arrangements, performance would have been lower.

Important Risks: Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Security prices fluctuate in value depending on general market and economic conditions and the

data.title: Garden Of EDEN: Despite Turbulent Greenland Geopolitics, Denmark Is A Buy
data.description:
Thesis

The iShares MSCI Denmark ETF (EDEN) offers attractive overall fundamentals, and Denmark’s economy remains one of the strongest economies in Europe. Low sovereign debt, a relatively young population (by European standards), reliance on a national currency, and other factors leave me feeling bullish about Denmark’s near- and medium-term prospects.

Given the fundamentals and macro tailwinds, I assign EDEN a buy rating. The issue with Greenland certainly is a threat and could upend markets globally. Yet in terms of the direct impact, Greenland is currently a drain on Denmark financially speaking, which would mitigate some of the damage should Copenhagen lose the island. I don’t expect the Trump Administration to take Greenland by force, but either way, belligerence will likely lead to Europe striving to depend less on America.

Rally Round Denmark

President Trump’s surprising aggression towards Denmark and the EU as a whole is likely to fuel a circle-the-wagons response. The United States and the EU (plus the UK) had reached a general understanding and framework for a trade deal just months ago. Yet now a new round of tariff wars may be upon us. Trade isn’t the focus this time around, true, but the fallout from the Greenland spat could quickly sink markets and upend relations between close allies.

I believe we’re going to see a sustained cooling of relations between Europe and the United States. Of course, if the United States forcefully takes Greenland, relations are probably toast, and long-term decoupling would likely unfold. Even if the Trump Administration steps back, however, I expect to see a substantial push in Europe to increase self-sufficiency and to rebalance power to even the playing field.

This is more of a hunch or anecdote, but it seems to me that when certain countries get singled out amid geopolitical drama, there’s a tendency for allies to go out of their way to help them. With Denmark, in particular, under threat from the Trump Administration, I believe European allies will go out of their way to provide support. Both at the EU level and through bilateral relationships, I suspect Denmark will enjoy a fair bit of sympathy. This might result in favorable investments, trade, and more.

Still, while Denmark is the focus of this article, the covered ETF, and the current geopolitical battle, the likely changes stemming from the Greenland spat will cross many borders. Among other things, the Trump Administration is proving unpredictable and disruptive. That’s bad not only for governments but also for businesses and investors. In response to repeated escalations with the Trump Administration, I believe we’ll see the EU as a whole drifting further away from the United States, with improved self-sufficiency now being a primary and perhaps existential aim.

Certainly, the mood among the European public is souring. Only 16% of Europeans viewed the United States as an ally in a November poll, before the Greenland drama flared up. 20% view America as a rival or enemy. A December poll by Politico found similar results. 52% of German and 47% of French adults believe the United States creates problems, while only 22% of Germans and 29% of French adults believe they solve problems.

How Would The Loss Of Greenland Affect Markets, Denmark, and EDEN?

I don’t believe President Trump is serious about seizing Greenland. That said, the president has proven me wrong before and has surprised me, too. As such, I certainly wouldn’t rule out Greenland being seized by force. If this does occur, markets globally will almost certainly plummet. Safe haven assets, like gold, would likely surge as investors look for off-ramps. I expect European stock markets to be hit the hardest initially, but the United States would sink rapidly as well. I’d expect non-EU and non-USA assets to recover more quickly, and China in particular could look all the more attractive.

Denmark’s stock markets would almost certainly be among the hardest hit, initially, which would weigh EDEN down. Yet interestingly, the loss of Greenland itself wouldn’t have much of an impact on Denmark from the perspective of GDP, resources, etc. Greenland is actually a drain on Denmark, with Copenhagen sending around $1 billion in aid to Greenland while getting little (financially speaking) in return. Theoretically, Greenland is flush with natural resources, but the harsh weather, glaciers, and other factors result in that natural wealth being locked away. It may be decades or more until extracting natural resources in Greenland en masse is economically viable.

Greenland currently exports a fair bit of seafood, but the total value is less than $2 billion. At least in the here and now, losing Greenland strictly from the perspective of a spreadsheet might actually be an economic benefit. Of course, this doesn’t consider Greenland’s vast but hard-to-reach natural resources. Further, the likely market meltdown wouldn’t be due to losing Greenland, per se, but instead the resulting geopolitical fallout that could upend world order.

EDEN Fundamentals And Valuation Considerations

In order to provide context, I’ll compare EDEN to iShares MSCI Germany ETF (EWG), as Germany is often considered the strongest European economy. Comparing EDEN to the S&P 500 (SPY) provides further context. With that in mind, EDEN’s portfolio enjoys a 16.66 PE ratio (Morningstar), to EWG’s 15.28 and SPY’s 22.35. The Price/Sales ratio for EDEN weighs in at 1.44, which is quite reasonable compared to SPY’s 3.17 but a bit above EWG’s 1.22. These PE and PS ratios are higher than what you typically find in Southern Europe, but Denmark’s economy overall is more heavily focused on growth and technology.

EDEN’s portfolio is a bit more highly concentrated than I prefer, with the top 10 holdings making up more than 65% of the portfolio weight. There are 49 holdings, according to Seeking Alpha data. Given the high concentration in Novo Nordisk, which makes up +23% of the portfolio, I believe it’s important to take a quick look at the company individually.

Novo Nordisk Quick Fundamentals

Novo Nordisk (NYSE:NVO) is a major pharmaceutical company with its GLP-1 medications getting a lot of attention. These medications are proving highly effective at helping people lose weight. On an individual level, many people have a strong drive to get in shape. Governments too have generally been supportive of GLP-1 medications, as obese and overweight citizens typically require more healthcare spending. NVO is enjoying strong growth, with revenues up 16.64% (YOY). The net income margin is quite high at 32.88%, and the GAAP PE (TTM) ratio is reasonable at 16.04, compared to a sector median of 27.35.

I am not ready to offer a full individual rating for NVO, but the basic fundamentals look strong, and GLP-1 has the potential to turn into a reliable, profitable cash cow. The +20% portfolio weight does make me a bit nervous.

Stepping back to consider the whole portfolio, EDEN offers solid dividends at 2.60%. If my theory that Europe is going to shift inward is correct, I think we’ll see some European companies restrain dividends to free up resources to invest. I focus more on value than income, but I’d be careful about relying on EDEN as an income investment.

Costs are reasonable, by the way, with the adjusted cost ratio at .53%, but that is a bit above EWG’s .49%. Still, these international ETFs often have adjusted expense ratios exceeding .6%. Tracking errors are also quite high with international ETFs, with rates often topping 20%. EDEN is in decent shape with a 13.76% 1-year tracking error and an 11.81% 5-year tracking error. These numbers don’t wow me, but I’m not uncomfortable with them either.

All in all, the fundamentals here seem strong. The portfolio is reasonably diverse in terms of sectors, which I think is especially important for broad macro-focused theses, such as my own. Since I’m bullish regarding Denmark’s overall economy, and given reasonable valuation metrics, I assign EDEN a buy rating. We’ll probably see some dips in the weeks ahead, so long as Greenland remains a source of discontent, but that could create attractive entry points.

Why I Could Be Wrong

First, President Trump may make good on his threats to take Greenland by force, which would likely sink markets across the globe. Denmark and EDEN would take an especially big hit, I suspect. Even after markets stabilize, the fallout in this scenario would likely be a drag on the world economy for many years to come.

Second, my bullish sentiments are predicated on the idea that Europe will emphasize self-sufficiency going forward. Europe faces myriad challenges, however, and long-term repositioning could create short-term pain. This could affect markets, but more concerningly, if European leaders take their foot off the gas, long-term risks may continue to mount and grow more grave.

Key Takeaway: Denmark Is A Buy, Making Eden A Buy

Uncertainty is the only thing certain in these uncertain times. Denmark, Greenland, the EU, and wider Europe will have to confront a shake-up in global order. However, I believe these challenges could ultimately make Europe stronger by encouraging internal focus, renewed effort, and an existential need to improve self-sufficiency. Against this backdrop, Denmark’s prospects look especially good. EDEN, in turn, is attractive, with solid fundamentals and reasonable valuations.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: I'm Bullish On Diana Shipping After The Genco Proxy Fight (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

Diana Shipping Inc. (DSX) is a Greece-based dry bulk carrier hauling iron ore, coal, and grain around the world. The last time I wrote about it, I had the stock at a Hold: it looked cheap, but not cheap enough to justify a Buy while weak freight rates were steadily eating away at earnings power.

In retrospect, DSX’s momentum’s been on the right track, now almost par with the broader market (SP500). In this update, after reviewing Diana’s move to replace the Genco Shipping & Trading Limited (GNK) board in an escalating takeover fight, I’m leaning bullish on the shares as activism targets NAV upside through consolidation.

Diana Shipping: Forcing Strategic Change

With roughly 14.8% of Genco’s shares in hand, Diana Shipping's signaling that it has real skin in the outcome. After Genco’s board flatly rejected its unsolicited all-cash offer of $20.60 per share, the company escalated its campaign. Last week, it nominated six independent directors, putting forward a full slate for the board. As best as I can tell, it’s an activist play, carefully planned and aimed at seating a board willing to pursue the value-boosting deal logic Diana Shipping considers obvious.

The dry bulk market is still very fragmented, meaning there are hundreds of ship operators all competing for the same types of cargo, such as iron ore, coal, grains, and minor bulks. All things being equal, larger fleets have more bargaining power with charterers, can secure better long-term contracts, and, most importantly, can spread fixed costs.

Diana operates about 36 vessels, and they’re relatively young, with an average age under 12 years, while total capacity is roughly 4.1 million deadweight tons. And the assets are actually working. Utilization basically maxed out at 99.5% last quarter and by late last year, they’d already locked in about $149 million of contracted revenue. Moreover, the charter book is deliberately staggered, averaging $16,200 per day with durations of more than one year. Recent results and fixing activity reinforces the point.

As of November 12, 2025, (Diana has) secured USD 25.4 million of contracted revenues for 87% of the remaining ownership days of the year 2025 and have secured USD 118 million of contracted revenues for 50% of the ownership days of the year 2026. Finally, (Diana is) pleased to declare a quarterly cash dividend of $0.01 per common share with respect to the third quarter of 2025, totaling approximately USD 1.16 million. (please see dividend history stats below)."

Between July and November 2025, Diana Shipping fixed 14 vessels, with Ultramaxes securing average rates of $13,800 per day for roughly 333 days with Panamax, Kamsarmax and Post-Panamax vessels at about $12,900 per day for 331 days. And Capesize and Newcastlemax vessels at approximately $24,500 per day for 380 days.

All things considered, like I stated in the beginning, Genco rejected the proposal, saying it undervalued the company and involved execution risks. In response, Genco suggested a broad and undefined alternative in which Genco would acquire Diana through a reverse takeover using a mix of cash and stock. But it didn't provide any details about pricing, structure, or a premium. Now Diana turned down their idea, saying it was a distraction, and noted that Genco took more than six weeks to reply and never engaged in any discussions about valuation or deal structure.

So with negotiations effectively at an impasse, Diana’s focus turned to governance. Just look at its nominees, people like Gustave Brun-Lie, Jens Ismar, Quentin Soanes, industry veterans with long track records in dry bulk, finance, and law. It signals a board refresh with intent. And more importantly, it puts pressure on the company to seriously evaluate consolidation options. In the meantime, Genco’s board says it will review the nominees through its usual process and lay out its recommendation in the proxy statement ahead of the 2026 annual meeting, which has yet to be scheduled.

Plenty Of Value, Plenty To Go Wrong

At $1.98, DSX is trading at about 3.6x operating cash flow. That’s cheap. Especially when the broader growth backdrop is closer to 15x, and even its own normal multiple is around 7.6x.

Meanwhile, at about 0.44x book value, DSX is trading far below the market value of its ships, even after accounting for the fact that its debt level is meaningful, with long-term debt at nearly 57% of total capital. That discount exists despite the stock throwing off a near 20% operating cash flow yield.

That said, risks are all over the place. For one thing, if Chinese import demand keeps cooling, rates could get hit. At the same time, fleet supply is set to grow about 3.4% in 2026, which doesn’t help. Put those together and day rates could slide. If vessels then refix below the roughly $16,800 breakeven, you’re probably looking at cash burn.

Speaking of which, the cash flow statement is where the risk actually shows up.

Right now, DSX is still throwing off about $60 million of operating cash flow. Free cash flow is positive too, even though a big chunk of the fleet is fixed around the mid-$16,000s per day. That helps. And it comes from the aforementioned high utilization, staggered charters, and very light capex, which is partly funded by recent vessel sales. Still, that cushion isn’t permanent. If rates stay below breakeven for any length of time as contracts roll off, free cash flow flips negative pretty fast.

That risk is higher because capital is tied up in the Genco stake, and I don't see much room left to stretch the balance sheet further. I think with the current discounted price, the activist strategy needs to accelerate value realization. But if the dispute drags on, the company continues to suffer, and the path back toward NAV becomes slow and uncertain.

Final Takeaway

I’m upgrading DSX to a highly speculative Buy. I think there’s upside if the activists force consolidation and the stock rerates closer to NAV. At the same time, the downside looks cushioned, since a chunk of the cash flow is contracted and the assets have real value. So while it’s not risk-free, the risk/reward looks skewed the right way.

data.title: Occidental Petroleum: Higher Gas Prices, Dividend Hike & Divestiture Tailwinds Ahead
data.description:
I previously covered Occidental Petroleum Corporation (OXY) in September 2025, discussing how they had demonstrated their ability to drive improved capital efficiency, as observed in the lowered FY2025 capex and the raised FQ3'25 production guidance.

Combined with the reduction of per-barrel cost/the richer cash flows in H1'25, the accelerated deleveraging cadence, and the divestiture of non-core, largely non-operated assets in its US onshore business, allowing it to largely streamline its operations, I had reiterated my Buy rating near to its established floor at the $36s then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the OXY stock near the December 2025 floor of $38, attributed to the potential FQ4'25 outperformance arising from the higher gas spot prices (well balancing the lower oil prices) and the likely dividend hike sometime in early 2026.

This is on top of the accelerated deleveraging progress likely triggering the adj EPS outperformance and the healthier balance sheet from FQ1'26 onwards, thanks to the recently completed OxyChem divestiture.

OXY's Prospects Discussed Entering FQ4'25 & FY2026

Since my last Buy rating, OXY has already traded along the volatile Brent oil spot prices of between $60s and $65s since Q4'25, with the stock previously nearing my Buy Zones of $36s during the worst of the December 2025 selloff to the $38s.

This is an unsurprising development indeed, given its upstream-focused oil/gas operations at 85.6% of its YTD incomes (+13.6 points YoY), with the balance derived from the midstream operations.

Moving forward. OXY was set to report their FQ4'25 earnings performance on February 19, 2026; I will be discussing numerous important performance metrics for readers to focus on.

1. Realized Prices

Given that its upstream segment is highly sensitive to the fluctuating spot prices, readers may want to monitor OXY's:

  • realized oil prices in FQ4'25 against the $64.78 per barrel reported in FQ3'25 (+1.5% QoQ/-14% YoY),
  • realized NGL prices against the $19.60 per barrel in FQ3'25 (-5.3% QoQ/-4.2% YoY), and
  • realized natural gas prices against the $1.57 per MCF in FQ3'25 (+7.5% QoQ/+106.5% YoY).

These metrics are important indeed, since OXY's overall production capacity is split rather equally between oil at ~50.9% and NGL/natural gas at ~49% in FQ3'25, with the latter recently enjoying higher spot prices at an average price of approximately $3.74 per Mbtu in Q4'25 (+23.8% QoQ/+53.2% YoY).

This is thanks to the increased deployment of gas-powered power plants at a time of data center boom - a trend that has benefitted many players, including Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) and Energy Transfer LP (ET), as numerous hyperscalers and data center REITs pursue renewable energies and even nuclear reactors as their source of reliable/clean energy as part of their ESG strategies.

This tailwind may very well balance the headwinds observed in the oil segment, attributed to the oversupply risks arising from the OPEC+ raised output in 2025 and the growing US crude oil output by +2.5% YoY as of October 2025, since these two players comprise over half of the global oil producers by capacity (US at 22% and OPEC+ at 35%).

With oversupply presenting a real risk entering 2026, it is unsurprising that the US Energy Information Administration [EIA] already expects "the Brent spot price will average $56/b in 2026 and $54/b in 2027, compared with an average of $69/b in 2025."

2. Capital Efficiency

Otherwise, readers must note that OXY is one of the more capital-efficient E&P players, as observed in the majority of its U.S. Onshore Unconventional Gross Operated Inventory, boasting a low breakeven price range at below <$50 per barrel.

This is compared to the "average breakeven price was $62/b in the Permian Midland Basin and $64/b in the Permian Delaware Basin, the two largest basins in the Permian, according to data from a Dallas Fed Energy survey."

This implies OXY's ability to remain profitable on a Free Cash Flow basis despite the potential oil oversupply risks entering 2026.

The same has been similarly observed in the lower oil & gas revenues of $5.4B in FQ3'25 (-5% YoY), with the higher overall production of 1,227 MBOE/D (+3.4% YoY) and the higher natural gas prices unable to balance the lower realized oil prices as discussed above.

This is, albeit, with FQ3'25 bringing forth a more than decent Free Cash Flow generation at $1.47B (-2.6% YoY) despite the higher capex at $1.72B (+5.5% YoY) and the notable top-line erosion, with it lending credence to my Free Cash Flow conjecture above.

The same has been observed in OXY's FY2026 production growth guidance of up to +2% YoY alongside the FY2026 capital spending guidance of $6.5B at the midpoint (+3.1% YoY, after omitting the OxyChem $900M capex), with these numbers lending credence to their efficient capital allocation efforts thus far.

3. Balance Sheet Health

At the same time, readers must note that OxyChem's $9.7B divestiture recently completed in early January 2026 has contributed to the accelerated deleveraging progress below OXY's target of <$15B at an Expected Post-Transaction Debt Balance of $14.3B (-42.5% YoY from FQ3'24 net debt of $24.87B).

This development is likely to trigger an "opportunistic multi-year share repurchase program" beyond current levels of 1,003.1M (-0.7% QoQ), potentially nearer to FQ4'24 lows of 940.8M.

This is on top of OXY's potentially raised dividend payouts beyond the current levels of $0.96 per share, based on the last two hikes by +9.1% in February 2025 and +22.2% in December 2023.

This may potentially trigger an expansion in its forward dividend yield beyond the current levels of 2.25%, compared to the sector median of 3.82% and its direct oil/gas peers, including ConocoPhillips (COP) at 3.42%, XOM at 3.17%, Chevron (CVX) at 4.11%, and Devon (DVN) at 2.65%.

As a result of the accelerated deleveraging progress along with the realization of ">$350 MM of annual interest expense savings," I posit that FQ1'26 is likely to bring forth a sequentially healthier balance sheet along with an excellent adj EPS performance, with it building upon OXY's potential FQ4'25 outperformance arising from the higher gas spot prices, as discussed in the first section.

OXY Remains a Great Buy Upon a Dip

For now, OXY has been seemingly well defended at the Q4'25 trading floor of $39s, with the stock currently recovering along the higher Brent/gas prices albeit seemingly unable to break out of the $44s resistance levels in January 2026.

Despite the stock price volatility, I am of the opinion that OXY remains fairly valued at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 18.84x and FWD Price/Cash Flow valuations of 3.90x, compared to its pre-pandemic mean of 20.93x/10.07x and its oil/gas peers, including:

  • upstream - COP at 15.59x/6.17x,
  • diversified - XOM at 18.69x/9.85x, and
  • diversified - CVX at 22.87x/9.02x,
  • aside from upstream - DVN at 9.05x/3.50x, respectively.

When compared to its peers, it is apparent that OXY remains cheaply valued at my estimated Enterprise Value to Proven Reserve ratio of 15.52x as well.

This is based on OXY's Enterprise Value of $71.58B by the time of writing and the 2024 proved reserves of 4.61M BOE (+15.8% YoY), against COP at 17.74x, XOM at 29.27x, CVX at 38.19x, and DVN at 14x, using the same methodology.

As a result of the potential FQ4'25 outperformance arising from the higher gas spot prices, the likely dividend hike in early 2026, along with the accelerated deleveraging progress likely triggering the adj EPS outperformance and the healthier balance sheet from FQ1'26 onwards, I am cautiously reiterating my Buy rating for the OXY stock, albeit with the caveat that investors observe the stock price movement for a little longer.

My caution is attributed to the seemingly reversing momentum after the recent inability to break out of the $44 resistance levels, with the stock potentially returning some of its recent gains to bounce off the $39 support levels.

Given the excellent dip-buying opportunity upon the -8.6% correction from current levels and the consequently richer dividend yields at approximately ~2.4%, some patience may be quite prudent in the meantime.
